subject: The Better Hand: Should You Open With 4 Wilds? 🃏
previewText: The answer might surprise your whole table.
body: Hey {{ person.first_name|default:'friend' }}, Here's this week's Better Hand: one tip to sharpen your game before the weekend. Would you meld 4 wild cards just to meet the point requirement? It's tempting. With two Jokers and two Twos you hit 140 points, grab 4 cards from the pack, and you're off to the races. But here's the thing: those wilds are worth more in your hand than on the table. An open meld of wild cards with that many cards to a canasta is a liability. You don't know if your partner will have more, nor when you will get more from the pack. Every time you have a wild card meld open and the chance to pick the pack, you face a tough call. Do you grab the pack, or try to salvage that meld into a canasta before the round ends? The penalty is far too big to guess wrong. Most experienced players wait for 5. Some won't go below 6. The math favors patience here. Try this weekend: If you draw 4 wilds early, resist the urge to meld. Hold them. See how many more come your way before committing. Weekend game night? 10% off with code CANASTA10 at checkout: Shop All7s Warmly, Ramon P.S. We share tips like this every week on Instagram. Come say hi: https://instagram.com/all7sgames

subject: The Better Hand: Stop Emptying Your Hand So Fast 🃏
previewText: Your partner melded. You matched everything. Big mistake.
body: Hey {{ person.first_name|default:'friend' }}, Here's this week's Better Hand. One tip to sharpen your game before the weekend. **Do you match every meld your partner puts down?** It feels productive. Partner melds kings, you throw yours on. Partner melds 8s, you add yours. Before you know it, you're holding 3 cards and praying for a good draw. The problem: an empty hand can't pick the pack. And picking the pack is how you win games. Keep cards in reserve. Even if you could meld them. Having 8-10 cards in hand gives you options. It keeps your opponents guessing. It gives you pack access. Save them for the end of the round. **Try this weekend:** Next time your partner melds, add one card max. Keep the rest. See how much more control you have mid-game. Weekend game night? 10% off with code CANASTA10 at checkout: Shop All7s Warmly, Ramon

subject: The Better Hand: Are You Tracking Aces and Sevens? 🃏
previewText: Two cards that can wreck your whole hand if you're not careful.
body: Hey {{ person.first_name|default:'friend' }}, Here's this week's Better Hand. One tip to sharpen your game before the weekend. **How many aces are you holding right now?** Aces and sevens are sneaky. They're worth points, sure. But hold too many and you're sitting on a penalty bomb if someone goes out. The rule of thumb: never hold more than two aces or two sevens unless you're actively building those canastas. Three aces in hand with no meld on the table? Start discarding. Good players track these across the whole table. Count what's been melded, what's been discarded. If the player to your right discards a 5th ace and you're holding 2, you just got yourself 2500 points. **Try this weekend:** Before each discard, keep count of the aces and sevens on the table. Make your discard decision based on what you see, not just what you're holding. Weekend game night? 10% off with code CANASTA10 at checkout: Shop All7s Warmly, Ramon

subject: The Better Hand: What to Throw Away First 🃏
previewText: Your early discards set up everything that comes after.
body: Hey {{ person.first_name|default:'friend' }}, Here's this week's Better Hand. One tip to sharpen your game before the weekend. **What's the first card you discard?** Most players toss whatever feels useless. But your early discards matter more than you think. Here's the move: shed high-value singles first. That lone king, that random queen with no pair. Get rid of them early when the pack is small and less attractive. Why? If someone catches you with high singles when the hand ends, those points hurt. A lone king is 10 points against you. A lone ace is 20. Early in the hand, nobody's grabbing a 3-card pack for one king. Later, they might. Keep your pairs. Keep your low cards. Dump the expensive loners. **Try this weekend:** In round one, look at your hand and find the most expensive card that has no pair. That's your first discard. Weekend game night? 10% off with code CANASTA10 at checkout: Shop All7s Warmly, Ramon

subject: The Better Hand: Check for a Special Hand Before You Do Anything 🃏
previewText: 30 seconds of thinking could save your whole round.
body: Hey {{ person.first_name|default:'friend' }}, Here's this week's Better Hand. One tip to sharpen your game before the weekend. **Do you check for special hands before your first meld?** Most players pick up their cards, sort by rank, and start planning melds. But before you do anything else, scan for a special hand. A special hand (pairs, garbage, all clean) is worth big points and ends the round instantly. If you're 3-4 cards away from one, it might be worth chasing instead of melding normally. The key: you have to decide early. Once you start melding, special hands are off the table. That first meld is a commitment. Make sure it's the right one. Spend 30 seconds before your first turn. Count your pairs. Check if you're close. If not, play normally. If yes, hold off on melding and see what develops. **Try this weekend:** Before your first meld of every hand, count your pairs. If you have 8 or more, consider going for it. Weekend game night? 10% off with code CANASTA10 at checkout: Shop All7s Warmly, Ramon

subject: The Better Hand: Are You Watching the Discards? 🃏
previewText: That pile of thrown cards is telling you everything.
body: Hey {{ person.first_name|default:'friend' }}, Here's this week's Better Hand. One tip to sharpen your game before the weekend. Are you paying attention to every discard? Most players watch the pile when it's their turn. They glance at the top card and decide. But the real information comes from watching every single throw, including your partner's. When your opponent discards a jack twice, they probably don't have a third. That makes jacks safer for you to throw. When your partner tosses a six early, they're telling you something about their hand. Pay attention. The discard pile is a running scoreboard of information. Who threw what. When they threw it. What they're holding back. Every card tells a story if you're watching. Try this weekend: Pick one opponent and track their last three discards in your head. You'll start seeing patterns you never noticed. Weekend game night? 10% off with code CANASTA10 at checkout: Shop All7s Warmly, Ramon

subject: The Better Hand: Protect the Pack With Your Own Cards 🃏
previewText: Sometimes the smartest play is throwing a card you want to keep.
title: Why Would You Throw Away a Card You Need?
body:
You have four 8s on the table and two more in your hand. Six total. One card away from a canasta.

So why would you throw an 8?

Because protecting the pack matters more than padding your meld. If you hold both 8s and the opponent to your left needs to discard, they might toss something that lets the other team grab the pile. Your extra 8 sitting in hand does nothing to stop that.

But an 8 on the discard pile? Nobody can pick that. Your team already has the rank locked down. It is a dead card to everyone else.

Think of it this way. A meld of 5 with a protected pack beats a meld of 6 with the pack gone. Points on the table mean nothing if your opponents scoop up 20 cards and bury you.

The best players throw cards that look generous but cost them nothing. That spare 8 is your shield.
challenge: Next time you have 4 or more of a rank melded and extras in hand, throw one to protect the pack instead of hoarding it.

subject: The Better Hand: Close That Canasta Before It's Too Late 🃏
previewText: A mixed canasta now beats a clean canasta never.
title: Is Chasing Clean Worth the Risk?
body:
You have five 10s and a wild card on the table. Six cards. One more and you close a mixed canasta for 300 points.

But you keep waiting. Maybe another 10 shows up. Maybe you can pull the wild off and close it clean for 500.

Meanwhile your partner is down to three cards. The opponents are counting theirs. Someone is about to go out.

Close it. Right now.

A mixed canasta in hand is 300 points you bank today. A clean canasta you never finish is zero. Worse than zero if your opponent catches you mid-build and goes out while you are sitting on an open meld.

Watch your partner's card count. If they are at five or fewer cards, stop chasing perfection. Drop the wild, close the canasta, move on. You can chase clean canastas when the timing is safe. When the round feels tight, grab what you can.
challenge: This weekend, if you have 6 cards in a meld and your partner has 5 or fewer cards, close it mixed. Do not wait for clean.
